idioms.definition: To make a bad situation worse, especially by doing or saying something that intensifies an argument or conflict
examples: Bringing up old grievances during the argument only added fuel to the fire.
His dismissive reply added fuel to the fire and the dispute escalated quickly.
Rather than calming things down, the announcement added fuel to the fire.
idioms.usageTip: Also found as 'add fuel to the flames'. Used when someone's action or words make a conflict worse rather than helping to resolve it. Often a warning or criticism.

idioms.definition: To make neither a profit nor a loss; to reach a point where income equals expenditure
examples: The company barely broke even in its first year of trading.
We need to sell at least two hundred tickets just to break even.
After paying all the bills, she just about broke even on the event.
idioms.usageTip: Common in business and finance contexts. 'Break-even point' is the noun form. Implies that no money is made, but none is lost either – a neutral financial outcome.

idioms.definition: Excessive official rules, regulations, and bureaucratic procedures that cause unnecessary delays
examples: Getting planning permission involves so much red tape that it took over a year.
Small businesses often struggle with the amount of red tape involved in expanding abroad.
The humanitarian aid was delayed by red tape at the border.
idioms.usageTip: Often used critically to describe frustrating bureaucracy. It's an uncountable noun: 'so much red tape', not 'a red tape'. The term comes from the practice of tying official documents with red ribbon.

idioms.definition: A fairly large or impressive amount of money
examples: She sold her flat for a tidy sum and used the money to travel.
Winning that contract is worth a tidy sum to the company.
He's been saving for years and has put aside quite a tidy sum.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used with 'a' or 'quite a'. The word 'tidy' here means 'considerable' rather than 'neat'. Implies that the amount is impressive without being extravagant.

idioms.definition: The skills, knowledge, equipment, or techniques needed to do a particular job effectively
examples: A good chef knows the tools of the trade – from knife skills to timing.
Learning to negotiate is one of the key tools of the trade for a sales professional.
The apprentice spent her first month mastering the tools of the trade.
idioms.usageTip: Can refer to both physical tools and non-physical skills or knowledge. Often used to emphasise that expertise in a profession requires specific, learned capabilities.

idioms.definition: The people who are in authority or in charge of an organisation, system, or country
examples: The powers that be have decided to restructure the entire department.
We'd love to expand the programme, but the powers that be have frozen the budget.
No one dared question the decision – the powers that be had spoken.
idioms.usageTip: Slightly formal or ironic in tone. Implies that the decision-makers are distant or that their authority is unquestioned. Can be used with mild frustration or deference.

fillInExercises.sentence: His sarcastic comment in the meeting just ___ – the client was already upset.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Add fuel to the fire' means to make an already bad situation significantly worse.

fillInExercises.sentence: The café didn't make a profit in its first quarter, but at least it managed to ___.
options: name the tools of the trade
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Break even' means to reach a point where income equals costs, resulting in neither a profit nor a loss.

fillInExercises.sentence: Setting up the import business was a nightmare because of all the ___ involved.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Red tape' refers to excessive bureaucratic rules and procedures that cause delays.

fillInExercises.sentence: The painting sold at auction for ___ – far more than the owner had expected.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A tidy sum' describes a fairly large or impressive amount of money.

fillInExercises.sentence: Knowing how to read a balance sheet is one of the essential ___ for any finance professional.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The tools of the trade' are the skills, knowledge, or equipment needed to perform a job well.

fillInExercises.sentence: We want to update the policy, but ___ have blocked every proposal so far.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The powers that be' refers to the people in authority who make decisions, often used when those decisions are frustrating or hard to challenge.

lines.speaker: Yasmin
lines.text: How did the launch event go? Did you at least manage to [break even|break even]?

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: Just about. Ticket sales covered the costs. But the build-up was exhausting – so much [red tape|red tape] to get the venue licence approved in time.

lines.speaker: Yasmin
lines.text: I bet. And I heard there was some drama with the catering supplier?

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: Yes. My business partner criticised them publicly, which just [added fuel to the fire|add fuel to the fire]. They almost pulled out entirely.

lines.speaker: Yasmin
lines.text: Ouch. Did the event make a good impression on investors at least?

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: Yes – one investor mentioned [a tidy sum|a tidy sum] she'd be willing to put in if we hit our targets next quarter.

lines.speaker: Yasmin
lines.text: That's fantastic news. And [the powers that be|the powers that be] at the funding body – are they on board?

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: Getting there. Knowing [the tools of the trade|the tools of the trade] when it comes to grant applications has really helped us navigate their requirements.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: Thanks for agreeing to meet. I'm struggling to get my consultancy off the ground. There's so much [red tape|red tape] just to register properly.

lines.speaker: Neil
lines.text: That's normal at the start. Be patient – it gets easier once you know your way around the system. That knowledge is one of [the tools of the trade|the tools of the trade].

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: I know. I also had a falling-out with a potential partner last week, and I'm worried I [added fuel to the fire|add fuel to the fire] by sending an angry email.

lines.speaker: Neil
lines.text: Try to resolve it calmly. Burning bridges is never worth it. Financially, are you managing to [break even|break even]?

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: Yes, just. I landed a contract last month worth [a tidy sum|a tidy sum], which helped a lot.

lines.speaker: Neil
lines.text: Good. Have you considered applying for the enterprise grant? [The powers that be|The powers that be] at the regional office are quite supportive of new consultancies.

lines.speaker: Rosa
lines.text: I hadn't thought of that. I'll look into it straight away. Thank you.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: to make a bad situation worse
to make neither a profit nor a loss
excessive official rules and procedures that cause delay
a fairly large amount of money
the skills or equipment needed to do a job
the people in authority who make decisions

quizExercises.sentence: The project is still in the early stages, but we hope to at least ___ by the end of the financial year.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Break even' means to reach a point where revenue exactly covers costs, resulting in no profit or loss.

quizExercises.sentence: Bringing up salary differences in the middle of a team argument really ___.
options: showed the tools of the trade
quizExercises.explanation: 'Add fuel to the fire' means doing something that makes an existing conflict or bad situation significantly worse.

quizExercises.question: Which scenario is a classic example of 'red tape'?
options: A manager who gives clear, simple instructions to their team
A startup spending months filling in forms and waiting for approvals before it can legally operate
A customer who receives fast and efficient service
quizExercises.explanation: 'Red tape' refers to excessive bureaucratic procedures and regulations that create unnecessary delays, as in the case of a startup struggling with approvals.

quizExercises.question: Which sentence uses 'the powers that be' correctly?
options: The powers that be decided to close the regional office without consulting staff.
The powers that be helped me carry my bags to the car.
I used the powers that be to finish the report on time.
quizExercises.explanation: 'The powers that be' refers to those in authority who make decisions, often used when those decisions affect others significantly.
